county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1818 from Montgomery County. County seat is Prattville. Named for Autauga Creek. Significant cotton agriculture with Daniel Pratt's industrial center at Prattville. Part of the central Alabama plantation belt.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Prattville handles wills, estates, and marriage licenses. Autauga County was formed from Montgomery County in 1818 and has relatively intact records from that date. Daniel Pratt's cotton gin factory in Prattville (1838) generated extensive business and employment records — check ADAH and the Prattville Public Library. The county had a large enslaved population tied to cotton agriculture; check slaveholder tax records and estate inventories at ADAH. FamilySearch has microfilmed Autauga County deed books, probate records, and marriage registers. The Autauga Genealogical Society maintains surname files and local research guides. For pre-1818 records, search Montgomery County.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Autauga County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1809 from Washington County — one of Alabama's earliest counties. County seat Bay Minette (since 1901, previously Daphne). Alabama's largest county by area. Gulf Coast county with French, Spanish, and British colonial heritage. The Poarch Band of Creek Indians has a reservation in the county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Bay Minette. Baldwin County is Alabama's largest county by area and has a unique multicultural history — French, Spanish, British, and American jurisdictions all governed the area before Alabama statehood. Early records from Fort Condé (Mobile) and Spanish West Florida land grants at ADAH and NARA. Baldwin County was originally part of Washington County (1809). The county seat moved from Daphne to Bay Minette in 1901. Check the Baldwin County Heritage Museum in Elberta and the Fairhope Public Library for local collections. FamilySearch has microfilm of deeds, probate, and marriages. The Poarch Band of Creek Indians has tribal records relevant to Native American ancestry in southern Baldwin County. Federal land grant records at the BLM General Land Office are essential for early settlers.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Baldwin County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.record_losses: 1856 courthouse fire destroyed many early records; additional losses during Civil War
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1832 from Pike County. County seat Clayton. Black Belt plantation county with major cotton production. Eufaula was a prosperous antebellum river town on the Chattahoochee. Courthouse fire in 1856 destroyed many early records.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Clayton. The 1856 courthouse fire destroyed many early records, making this a burned county — always check ADAH and FamilySearch microfilm for surviving copies. Barbour County was a major Black Belt plantation county; enslaved persons records, estate inventories, and slaveholder tax records at ADAH are critical for African American research. Clayton was the boyhood home of several Alabama governors. The county was heavily contested during Reconstruction. Check the Eufaula Heritage Association for records of antebellum mansions and families. The Shorter Mansion and Eufaula Pilgrimage archives document planter families. For pre-1832 records, check Pike County.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Barbour County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.record_losses: 1856 courthouse fire destroyed deed books and probate records prior to that date
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1818 from Monroe and Montgomery counties. County seat Centreville. Important antebellum iron manufacturing area (Brierfield Ironworks, Tannehill). Courthouse fire in 1856 destroyed deed books and probate records.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Centreville. The 1856 courthouse fire destroyed deed books and probate records — for pre-1856 records, check ADAH microfilm and FamilySearch. Bibb County was an important antebellum iron manufacturing center; the Brierfield Ironworks (now a state park) has related industrial records. The county's Cahaba River valley supported both plantation agriculture and mining operations. Tannehill Ironworks was also significant for Civil War-era production. The Cahaba plantation and enslaved persons records at ADAH document African American families. FamilySearch has microfilmed surviving deed books and probate records from 1856 restart.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1818 from Cherokee and Creek Cession. County seat Oneonta. Appalachian foothill county with Union sympathies during Civil War. Covered bridges are a county landmark. Smaller farms predominated over plantation agriculture.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Oneonta. Blount County records are relatively intact from 1818. The county was a Unionist stronghold during the Civil War — many residents opposed secession, and some served in Union forces. This political division means military records should be checked on both sides. The county has strong Appalachian settlement patterns with families from Tennessee, Georgia, and the Carolinas. Palisades Park and Covered Bridge records at the Blount County Memorial Museum document community history. The county's mountainous terrain limited large-scale plantation agriculture, resulting in smaller farms and fewer enslaved persons compared to Black Belt counties. FamilySearch has microfilmed deeds, probate, and marriage records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Blount County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1866 from Barbour, Macon, Montgomery, and Pike counties. County seat Union Springs. Major Black Belt plantation county with very high enslaved population percentage before the Civil War. Active Freedmen's Bureau presence during Reconstruction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Union Springs. Created in 1866 from parts of Barbour, Macon, Montgomery, and Pike — for earlier records, check all four parent counties. Bullock County was a major Black Belt plantation county with one of Alabama's highest percentages of enslaved persons before the war. The Freedmen's Bureau operated actively in Bullock County during Reconstruction — check NARA and FamilySearch digitized Freedmen's Bureau records. Union Springs was a prosperous antebellum town; check estate inventories and tax records at ADAH for planter families. The county has significant African American church records from the postwar period. FamilySearch microfilm covers deeds and marriages.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1819 from Conecuh and Monroe counties. County seat Greenville. Agricultural county on the Louisville and Nashville Railroad. Cotton and later peanut farming dominated the economy.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Greenville. Butler County records are largely intact from 1819. The county was formed from Conecuh and Monroe counties. Greenville was a major stop on the Louisville and Nashville Railroad, making it an important commercial center — check railroad employment records and town business directories. The county has strong agricultural records tied to cotton production. Check the Butler County Historical Society and the Greenville-Butler County Public Library for local family files. Confederate military records are important here; many Butler County men served in Alabama infantry regiments. FamilySearch has microfilmed deed books, probate, and marriage records. ADAH holds county tax records documenting property and enslaved persons.

county_metadata.record_losses: 1865 courthouse fire at Jacksonville during Civil War; records rebuilt from copies where possible
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1832 from Creek Cession as Benton County; renamed Calhoun 1858. County seat Anniston (formerly Jacksonville until 1899). 1865 courthouse fire at Jacksonville destroyed many records. Anniston was a planned industrial city (iron/steel). Fort McClellan military installation (1917-1999).
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Anniston. The 1865 courthouse fire at Jacksonville (the original county seat) during the Civil War destroyed many early records. The county seat moved to Anniston in 1899. Calhoun County was originally called Benton County (renamed 1858). For pre-1865 records, check ADAH microfilm, FamilySearch, and surviving state copies. Anniston was founded as a planned industrial city in 1872 by Samuel Noble and Daniel Tyler — the Anniston City Land Company records document early residents. Fort McClellan (1917-1999) military records at NARA document service families. The Calhoun County seat change means records may be split between Jacksonville and Anniston courthouses. The Public Library of Anniston-Calhoun County has local history collections.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Calhoun County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1832 from Creek Cession. County seat LaFayette. Eastern Alabama county on the Chattahoochee River. Textile mill industry (West Point Manufacturing Company) and cotton agriculture. Strong cross-border connections with Georgia.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in LaFayette. Chambers County records are intact from 1832. The county was formed from Creek Cession lands after the Treaty of Cusseta. The textile mill industry dominated the eastern part of the county, particularly in Lanett, West Point, and Valley — West Point Manufacturing Company records document mill worker families. The county was part of the Chattahoochee River cotton corridor. Check enslaved persons records at ADAH for pre-war plantation families. The Chattahoochee Valley Historical Society in West Point has relevant collections. FamilySearch has microfilm of deeds, probate records, and marriages. BLM GLO land patents document original settlers.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Chambers County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1836 from Cherokee Cession after Indian removal. County seat Centre. Named for the Cherokee Nation who inhabited the area. Weiss Lake and Coosa River shaped settlement. Cherokee removal records at NARA are important for pre-1836 research.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Centre. Cherokee County records are intact from 1836, when it was formed from Cherokee Cession lands after Indian removal. The county's name honors the Cherokee Nation, and early records document the transition from Cherokee to white settlement. The Cherokee Removal records at NARA (especially the Henderson Roll of 1835) are essential for tracing Cherokee families who lived here. Weiss Lake and the Coosa River shaped settlement patterns. The Cherokee County Historical Museum in Centre has family files. FamilySearch microfilm covers deeds, probate, and marriages. For Cherokee ancestry, also check the Dawes Rolls and Guion Miller Roll.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Cherokee County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants
